title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026, approving minutes and permits, awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding. The committee also directed staff to develop $100/day fine language for illegal sewer discharges and approved construction service contracts for two road projects.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
CIP update
Street operations update
Utility operations update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Recycled base course production contract
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex
Military Road construction agreement
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project may be moved to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding in the past 4-5 years. He also discussed Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading for road settlement over sewer trenches, and Scoffield Avenue repair items with HOS Construction including manhole repairs and concrete pitting.

Staff reported spring road limit signs were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Crews performed dewatering at the Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to flooding concerns near Crane Meadows. The committee discussed the ongoing drainage issues in that area, with suggestions for a permanent pump solution. Spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from East Nick were completed. A culvert failure on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the water system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. The Kathleen lift station showed particularly high clear water discharge from suspected sump pump connections. The INI study with in-pipe technologies is planned to identify problem areas. Well 2 rehab at Carrie started today.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value reached nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has 6,100 customers total with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

The League of Wisconsin Municipalities drafted this resolution seeking improved transportation funding for local operations. Michael noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and transportation funding doesn't cover what the public expects. The resolution passed unanimously with recommendation to the village board.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons ($126,900 total). Michael demonstrated savings of approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 in savings across three 2026 projects. Contract awarded to King Gravel unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on penalty amounts for illegal sump pump and clear water discharges to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members distinguished between unknowing and willful violations, recommending starting at $100/day for first offenses. The committee voted unanimously to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex builder at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs temporary water/sewer service via long laterals through Outlot 1 until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring the street be reconstructed no later than 2029, at which point the property owner pays assessment for main extension. Motion passed unanimously.

The village will reimburse Rothschild for Weston's water and sewer utility work on Military Road (Worfman to Helin Street) at an estimated $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Water main relocation is needed due to storm sewer conflicts. Agreement approved unanimously.

For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA was selected at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 over MSA's $70,400 due to having full-time staff rather than a summer intern. Both approved unanimously.

actionItems: Award recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations at $100/day fines
Authorize Radkey Avenue temporary utility connection agreement with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for approximately $94,860
Award Alderson/Jelnik construction services to MSA at $37,315
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz at $73,730
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impacts at next meeting
Look at incorporating street lighting into Scoffield Avenue reconstruction project (2030 timeframe)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing concerns about gas pipeline easements, future building expansion, and groundwater protection.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Disclosure and recusal
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items
Adjournment

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline easement, which reduced fill costs significantly. Discussion addressed future expansion capacity (building can expand north), landscaping meeting current relaxed code standards, and placement of an alternate storage shed. The shed is proposed as an alternate bid item for evidence/overflow storage. Commissioner Zagami moved to recommend approval subject to staff conditions 2-5, which passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized worksheet similar to DOT standards will be used. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. The motion to approve the program passed unanimously, with an amendment to report findings to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.

discussions.item: April 2026 minutes, survey maps, and building permits
discussions.body: The April 13, 2026 meeting minutes, April 2026 staff-approved certified survey maps and site plans, and April 2026 building permits were all approved unanimously with minimal discussion.

discussions.item: Planning department monthly update and future items
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 PM. Commissioner discussion touched on potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications. Electric vehicle charging stations were mentioned as a future fall agenda item.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings, noting some zoning complaints have gone unresolved for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Village Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection program findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not completed wellhead protection conditional use process
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 PM
Staff to send email and calendar invite to commissioners for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning members. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received comprehensive presentations from department heads about the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center operations and budgets. No major policy decisions were made as this was primarily an informational session.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Health Department budget discussion
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting aye.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under section 2.04 subsection 5, covering Health Department, Social Services, and Veteran Services as direct county departments, plus North Central Health Care and ADRC as multi-county entities. He outlined statutory requirements including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing a new strategic plan by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE across four divisions: environmental health (1,200+ licenses), nursing/communicable disease, family health, and community health improvement. She highlighted the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first cohort of 2-year-olds, public health vending machines with free tick kits, and a new homeless system response position funded by the county and City of Wausau. Supervisor Cavelli asked about vending machine locations - one is at community partners campus, another believed to be at Health First.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who could not attend. The department has nearly 140 FTE handling child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. Budget is over $22 million with approximately $7 million from tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability among child welfare and youth justice families, noting significant numbers of participants had never had credit accounts, bank accounts, or formal leases.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his small but mighty team of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. Veterans in Marathon County receive $44 million annually in VA benefits, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include expanded surviving spouse benefits under the PACT Act, housing stability efforts with Center for Veterans Issues establishing local office space, and suicide prevention focus after 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Galindo noted appointment wait times reduced from 6 weeks to under 3 weeks after reaching full staffing.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county Aging Disability Resource Center serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. The ADRC provides one-stop aging and disability services, Meals on Wheels nutrition program, Medicare counseling, falls prevention, and entry point services for Family Care and IRIS programs. Ray noted severe financial challenges with federal Older Americans Act funding flat since 2024 while nutrition costs increased 40-45%, forcing meal and staffing reductions. Marathon County contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate full presentation before committee
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan information to next or following meeting
Correct next meeting date from May 29th to June 3rd at 3:00 PM
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested agenda items for future meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed ordinances regulating mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved various license renewals.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public comments - Jim Pitel
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Department reports (Administrative, Clerk, Finance, Police, Parks, Planning, Public Works, Technology)
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances: Mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, bicycles
Discussion on remote meeting attendance policy amendments
Appointments to committees and commissions
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs to justify the fees. Trustee Barb expressed support, noting that as population increases, new residents should help pay for park costs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously with motion by Lope Sorrell and second by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously with motion by Hermling and second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained this creates a reciprocal inspection agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and other municipalities. Fire inspections from any participating community will be recognized by all, reducing redundant inspections for food truck operators. One department inspects and places a sticker on the truck valid for all participating communities. Approved unanimously with motion by Lope and second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. There was confusion about whether the policy language matched what was voted on at the previous meeting. Trustees agreed to strike sentences requiring health, safety, or welfare justification for remote attendance, keeping the policy broad. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustee Hermling and Trustee Barb. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two no votes. Key requirements remaining: notify 12 hours in advance, camera must be on, no remote participation in closed sessions.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Both appointments were approved unanimously with motion by Hermling and second by Lopes. The board agreed that future committee/commission applications should be included in board packets for all trustees to review.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License
discussions.body: Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street applied for secondhand dealer licenses. Discussion clarified that state statutes require licensing for businesses buying and selling used personal property to protect against stolen merchandise. Chief noted this involves precious metals and gold with large dollar transactions. Approved unanimously with an amendment requiring completion of background check and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue location when the property owner chose to develop the land. Trustee Lisa Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 53 stalls now, with vendors able to keep their vehicles nearby. The board discussed that Mock Miller Park was considered but rejected due to lower visibility. Discussion was informational only with no action taken. The market operates at no financial loss to the village.

Michael Dolan reported on warranty work on Weston Avenue, Fuller, and Scoffield Avenue for settling pavement from previous projects. He addressed the engineering technician vacancy, noting it's a specialized skill set difficult to fill locally. Regarding Weston Avenue speed limits, a speed study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph; the matter will return to the board for decision once construction is complete.

discussions.item: Planning Department Update on Ringle Township Assistance
discussions.body: Jen reported that Ringle Township contacted the village for help with building inspections for tornado-damaged homes. Staff will prepare an intergovernmental service agreement for the Ringle board to allow village inspectors to assist. The comprehensive plan steering committee first meeting was scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm as a joint meeting with the plan commission.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 2nd Street spoke during public comment and again during the impact fee public hearing. He criticized the village for spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants rather than filling vacant engineering technician positions, questioned the administrator's accountability, complained that Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment, criticized lack of Kennedy Park information in the board packet, and argued the village lacks sufficient data to justify park impact fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance 26-008 approved creating reciprocal inspection agreement
Ordinance 26-009 amending standing committees approved
Ordinance 26-011 amending nuisances chapter approved
Ordinance 26-012 amending bicycle regulations (e-bikes) approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting sustainable transportation funding approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove health/safety/welfare justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle Township building inspection assistance
Weston Avenue speed limit issue to return to board after construction completion
Committee/commission applications to be included in future board packets
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 6:00 pm

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors approved committee appointments for the new term, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for severe weather debris following tornado and flood damage in the Ringle area. The fee reduction, prompted by the state emergency declaration, lowers disposal costs from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster-related waste through June 17th.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large executive committee members
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16 - Social services budget amendment
Resolution E17 - Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli and seconded by Supervisor Crims passed but was not unanimous. Supervisor Mask noted the system marked him absent but he wanted to be recorded as voting aye.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for 'our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent agenda. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Resolution 24-26 was moved by Supervisor Morash and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution D16 - Social services budget amendment
discussions.body: Resolution 25-26 was moved by Supervisor P and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution E17 - Landfill tipping fee modification
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that damaged Ringle, Kronenwetter, and other areas. Leonard explained the state waived $13.10/ton, Ringle waived $2.33/ton, and the county would reduce its portion to reach $36.94/ton (covering operational costs). He noted the county won't meet the $670,743.18 threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle will. Dane County's director warned against waiving fees entirely as it affected their FEMA reimbursement in the 1990s. Supervisor Rosenberg requested public comment be allowed. The board suspended rules unanimously to hear the resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board. Motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash. The resolution passed unanimously.

publicComment: By special request from Supervisor Rosenberg, two members of the public were permitted to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to fully waive fees for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle have waived their fees, and that Marathon County cleanup efforts were largely self-reliant through donated business and community labor. She mentioned one gentleman spent $40,000 on hauling expenses. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for those underinsured, noting approximately 44 homes affected and some insurance adjusters have not yet arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris to $36.94/ton through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Board may revisit tipping fee reduction at May meeting if extension beyond June 17th is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to recommend a limited fee waiver for tornado-affected properties, though the committee narrowed the waiver to only cover septic system reconnect fees. The meeting also featured department overview presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31st, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview - Solid Waste Department
Department overview - Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department
Department overview - Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation; Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning makes these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve and forward to county board, seconded by Supervisor Drew. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to those affected by non-declared disasters. Eric Vogel asked whether homeowner's insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees for septic systems (not other zoning or new system fees), seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The amendment passed. The amended motion to forward to county board passed, but was noted as not unanimous.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented on solid waste operations including the landfill's 45-year history, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. Marilyn Bend praised the department's work. Supervisor Brook asked about the clean water fund grant application; Hagen Busher said they must reapply for 2027 funding.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both county and city of Wausau. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generating $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt noted Tom's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service. The committee thanked him for his service.

actionItems: Forward rezoning approval for James Offer/David Tract property (urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward rezoning approval for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only septic system reconnect fees ($160) for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable
Conservation Planning and Zoning to present conservation topics at future summer educational meeting

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on e-motos, and authorized demolition proceedings for an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also approved a new fire inspection program for mobile food vendors and discussed parking regulations, deferring action to a future meeting.

agenda.item: Call to order, Pledge of Allegiance, and roll call
Public comment
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Code enforcement report
Fire EMS Safer report
Police department report
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82
Overnight parking regulations discussion
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location at a park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning whether parking on grass violates village code. He raised ADA compliance concerns about handicap parking placement on grass rather than asphalt. Senalt also questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, requesting more accountability updates on long-standing violations.

discussions.item: Department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire EMS, Police)
discussions.body: Roman/Audrey reported that enforcement-suspended cases may move back to pending status now that construction season has begun, and noted 3020 Mountain View Avenue will come off the code enforcement roll after a successful auction and new owner permits. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with new officers starting in May and January. Two SROs work summer zoning violations across three municipalities. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded, passed unanimously to acknowledge all three reports.

Committee reviewed final copy of amendments previously discussed, including tree limb height requirements over streets (14 and 10 feet) that were switched per prior direction. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey to recommend approval to the board of trustees. Passed unanimously.

Extended discussion focused on e-motos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting e-motos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns. Committee discussed electric scooters and whether restrictions would affect children's transportation. Chief Hunt noted officers need clear ordinances for enforcement and suggested language exempting 'kids play scooters' for discretion. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property, seconded by Sherwanka. Passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson initiated discussion noting Weston's 12-hour parking ban (4am-4pm) is far outside what surrounding municipalities have (typically 5-hour overnight windows). Public Works Director Michael explained the daytime restriction was implemented because plowing typically doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8am, and overnight bans frustrated residents. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating the restriction entirely, preferring uniformity with other municipalities and noting $10 citations help clear roads before plowing. Committee deferred action, asking Michael to identify specific problem areas that could be signed individually, to be revisited in July/August.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest parking lot where parishioners park on Sundays. Committee noted no accidents have occurred and questioned whether village should pay for crosswalks on request. Michael estimated basic crosswalk cost at $2,000, lighted system at $25-30,000. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive is willing to cover installation costs, recommend board approval; seconded by Sherwanka. Passed unanimously. Michael to contact Mount Olive about cost-sharing.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendor fire inspection amendment
discussions.body: Fire Marshal Marty explained new regional program where Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer departments can inspect food trucks, with one inspection valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own. Chief Hunt noted a recent inspection failed a vendor due to galvanized steel LP piping and improperly secured propane containers. Inspections are free and follow NFPA 54 standards. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing split addresses, owner agreed), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue duplex on wrong side numbering. Chief Hunt added Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski has nine separate addresses for an eight-unit building plus utility room. Motion made and seconded to recommend approval. Passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin demolition proceedings on abandoned structure that has been posted since 2021 and abandoned since approximately 2010-2014. Fire department previously declined a training burn due to unsafe conditions. Owners had housed chickens inside the structure. Property is currently for sale and owners are current on taxes. Demolition costs will be placed on tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location, questioning whether parking on grass violates village code, raising ADA compliance issues with handicap parking on grass, questioning the need for food truck fire inspections, and criticizing the lack of progress updates on long-standing code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended to board of trustees for approval
E-bike ordinance with e-moto prohibition recommended to board of trustees for approval
Michael to identify specific problem parking areas for potential signing, bring back in July/August
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church about covering crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for three properties recommended to board of trustees
Staff authorized to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7, 2025 at 5:00 PM

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Village of Weston Tourism Commission was scheduled to elect new officers and consider a $10,000 grant request for the Taste N Glow Balloon Fest. The meeting was set to address leadership positions for the commission and evaluate funding for a major annual event expected to draw 15,000 attendees to the area.

agenda.item: Election of Officers: Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary
Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 21, 2026
Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest

discussions.body: The commission was scheduled to elect leadership positions including Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary. Current commission members eligible for these positions include Ermeling, Frederick, Hodell, Jalowitz, and Zeyghami.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest
discussions.body: The commission was set to consider a $10,000 room tax grant request from Taste N Glow Balloon Fest for their annual two-day event scheduled for July 10-11, 2026 in Marathon City. The event expects approximately 15,000 attendees and 45 estimated room nights, with funds to be used for advertising, potential shuttles, and lodging expenses for balloon pilots and crew members.

publicComment: Public comment period was included on the agenda.

Expected to consider approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Scheduled to vote on $10,000 grant request for Taste N Glow Balloon Fest

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Finance & Human Resource Committee was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026 to review April financial reports including budget status and TIF district details, as well as receive a presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This routine monthly meeting was set to provide fiscal oversight of village finances and departmental operations.

April Budget Status Report
TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Legal Details for April
Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget

discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to acknowledge the April 2026 financial report for all funds. According to the attached report, general fund revenue stood at 70.3% of budget while expenditures were at 40.7% of budget, with staff monitoring several line items that may exceed their budgets.

discussions.item: TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the monthly Tax Increment Financing district reports for TIF 1 and TIF 2. These reports provide ongoing monitoring of the village's tax increment financing districts.
The committee was expected to acknowledge the legal details report for April, providing oversight of the village's legal expenditures.

discussions.item: Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to receive an educational presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This follows a similar presentation format from the previous month when Planning & Development operations were reviewed.

title: Parks Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Parks and Recreation Committee was scheduled to meet on May 26, 2026 to receive updates on Kennedy Park and the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course, and to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center, graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail, and kayak launch signage. The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Public Comments
Written Comments
Kennedy Update
Yellowbank Disc Golf Course Update
Discussion and possible action Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Discussion and possible action Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Approve the Minutes
Audit of the Bills
Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27 (Action Requested)
Recommendation for Preliminary 2026-27 Budget (Action Requested)
2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update (Action Requested)
Activities & Athletics Code (Action Requested)
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)
Riverview RFP Approval (Action Requested)
Adjourn

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility

Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
